rules.title: Adjectival Pronouns - Overview
rules.text: Adjectival pronouns in Slovak decline like adjectives through all 7 cases and agree with nouns in gender (masculine, feminine, neuter), number (singular, plural), and case. They include demonstrative (this, that), possessive (my, your, his, her), interrogative (which, whose), relative (which, whose), and possessive reflexive (one's own) pronouns. Unlike substantive pronouns, these modify nouns rather than replace them.
examples: Demonstrative: tento dom (this house), tá žena (that woman)
Possessive: môj brat (my brother), tvoja sestra (your sister)
Interrogative: ktorý muž? (which man?), čí dom? (whose house?)
Relative: muž, ktorý pracuje (the man who works)
Reflexive possessive: svoj dom (one's own house)

rules.title: Possessive Pronouns - Complete Declension Table
rules.text: All possessive pronouns showing ownership. Note: 'jeho' (his/its), 'jej' (her), and 'ich' (their) are invariable and never change form.
examples: Môj brat je tu - My brother is here
Vidím svojho brata - I see my (own) brother
Jeho dom je veľký - His house is big (invariable)
V našom dome - In our house
S vaším otcom - With your father

rules.title: Demonstrative Pronouns - Complete Declension Table
rules.text: Demonstrative pronouns pointing to objects at different distances: 'ten' (that), 'tento' (this), 'tamten' (that over there).
examples: Ten muž je môj priateľ - That man is my friend
Tento dom je nový - This house is new
V tomto dome - In this house
Tamten dom je veľký - That house over there is big
S tým mužom - With that man

rules.title: Interrogative Pronouns - Complete Declension Table
rules.text: Interrogative pronouns used to ask questions: 'ktorý' (which, what) and 'čí' (whose).
examples: Ktorý muž? - Which man?
Ktorú knihu chceš? - Which book do you want?
V ktorom dome bývate? - In which house do you live?
Čí je to dom? - Whose house is it?
Čiu knihu čítaš? - Whose book are you reading?
S ktorým priateľom ideš? - With which friend are you going?

rules.title: Relative Pronouns - Complete Declension Table
rules.text: Relative pronouns used to introduce relative clauses: 'ktorý' (who, which, that) and 'čí' (whose). They decline identically to interrogative pronouns.
examples: Muž, ktorý pracuje - The man who works
Žena, ktorú vidím - The woman whom I see
Dom, v ktorom bývam - The house in which I live
Priateľ, s ktorým idem - The friend with whom I go
Muž, čí dom je veľký - The man whose house is big
Dieťa, čiu matku vidím - The child whose mother I see

rules.title: Key Usage Rules - Possessive Reflexive 'svoj'
rules.text: The reflexive possessive 'svoj' (one's own) is special because it refers back to the subject of the sentence, regardless of person or number. Use 'svoj' when the possessor is the subject; use 'jeho/jej/ich' when referring to someone else's possession.
examples: Vidím svoj dom - I see my (own) house
Vidíš svoj dom - You see your (own) house
Vidí svoj dom - He/she sees his/her (own) house
Vidíme svoj dom - We see our (own) house
Vidím jeho dom - I see his house (someone else's)
Hovorí o svojom bratovi - He talks about his (own) brother
Hovorí o jeho bratovi - He talks about his (someone else's) brother

rules.title: Key Usage Rules - Invariable Third Person Forms
rules.text: Unlike other possessive pronouns, third person forms 'jeho' (his/its), 'jej' (her), and 'ich' (their) NEVER change regardless of case, gender, or number of the noun they modify. They remain constant in all situations.
examples: Nominative: Jeho brat je tu - His brother is here
Genitive: Dom jeho brata - His brother's house
Dative: Dám to jeho bratovi - I'll give it to his brother
Accusative: Vidím jeho brata - I see his brother
Locative: V jeho dome - In his house
Instrumental: S jeho bratom - With his brother
Jej sestra, jej sestry, jej sestre - Her sister (all cases use 'jej')
Ich rodičia, ich rodičov, ich rodičom - Their parents (all cases use 'ich')

rules.title: Key Usage Rules - Animate vs Inanimate Accusative
rules.text: In accusative case, masculine pronouns have two forms: animate (for people and animals) uses the genitive form, while inanimate (for objects) uses the nominative form.
examples: Animate: Vidím toho muža - I see that man (toho = genitive form)
Animate: Vidím môjho brata - I see my brother (môjho = genitive form)
Animate: Ktorého psa máš? - Which dog do you have? (ktorého = genitive)
Inanimate: Vidím ten dom - I see that house (ten = nominative form)
Inanimate: Vidím môj dom - I see my house (môj = nominative form)
Inanimate: Ktorý dom? - Which house? (ktorý = nominative)

rules.title: Key Usage Rules - Personal vs Non-Personal Plural
rules.text: In nominative plural, masculine pronouns distinguish between personal (people) and non-personal (animals, objects). Personal forms typically end in -í, while non-personal end in -é.
examples: Personal: tí muži - those men (tí = personal)
Personal: moji bratia - my brothers (moji = personal)
Personal: ktorí študenti - which students (ktorí = personal)
Non-personal: tie domy - those houses (tie = non-personal)
Non-personal: moje psy - my dogs (moje = non-personal)
Non-personal: ktoré autá - which cars (ktoré = non-personal)

rules.title: Summary - Declension Patterns
rules.text: Quick reference guide showing which pronouns follow which declension patterns.

rows: Decline fully through all cases with gender agreement
Decline fully through all cases with gender agreement
Decline fully through all cases with gender agreement
Never change form regardless of case, gender, or number
Refers back to subject; used for all persons
